question_text: Which of the following is a natural method of contraception?
explanation: The rhythm method (periodic abstinence) is a natural method of contraception in which couples avoid coitus during the fertile period (day 10 to day 17) of the menstrual cycle when ovulation is expected. Other natural methods include coitus interruptus and lactational amenorrhoea. These methods do not use any drugs, devices, or surgical procedures.

elimination_hints.hint: Condoms are barrier methods, not natural methods. They involve use of a physical device.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying barrier methods as natural methods.

elimination_hints.hint: Oral pills are hormonal contraceptives, which are chemical methods, not natural.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing hormonal methods with natural methods.

elimination_hints.hint: IUDs are intra-uterine devices that need to be inserted into the uterus — they are device-based, not natural.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying device-based methods as natural.

question_text: In the rhythm method of contraception, couples avoid coitus during:
explanation: In the rhythm method (periodic abstinence), couples avoid coitus from day 10 to day 17 of the menstrual cycle. This is because ovulation typically occurs around day 14, and the ovum remains viable for about 1-2 days while sperms can survive for about 3 days. Thus, avoiding intercourse during this fertile window (day 10-17) prevents fertilisation.

options.text: Day 1 to day 7 of the menstrual cycle
Day 10 to day 17 of the menstrual cycle
Day 20 to day 28 of the menstrual cycle
Day 1 to day 5 of the menstrual cycle

elimination_hints.hint: Day 1-7 corresponds to the menstrual phase when the chances of conception are very low.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the menstrual phase with the fertile period.

elimination_hints.hint: Day 20-28 is the secretory/luteal phase when ovulation has already occurred and the ovum is no longer viable.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing the fertile window too late in the cycle.

elimination_hints.hint: Day 1-5 is the period of menstruation, which is not the fertile window.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking menstruation coincides with the fertile period.

question_text: Lactational amenorrhoea as a method of contraception is based on the principle that:
explanation: Lactational amenorrhoea is a natural method of contraception based on the fact that ovulation and menstrual cycles do not occur during the period of intense lactation following childbirth. The hormone prolactin, which stimulates milk production, also suppresses the release of gonadotropins (FSH and LH), thereby inhibiting ovulation. This method is effective only up to a maximum of six months following parturition.

options.text: Breastfeeding kills sperms in the female reproductive tract
Lactation prevents implantation of the embryo
Ovulation does not occur during intense lactation following parturition

elimination_hints.hint: Breastfeeding does not produce spermicidal effects; the mechanism involves hormonal suppression of ovulation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing spermicidal properties to breastfeeding.

elimination_hints.hint: Lactational amenorrhoea prevents ovulation itself, not implantation. Without ovulation, there is no ovum to fertilise.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the mechanism of action — ovulation is prevented, not implantation.

elimination_hints.hint: Breast milk does not contain spermicidal substances; the contraceptive effect is due to hormonal suppression of ovulation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inventing non-existent spermicidal properties of breast milk.

question_text: Which of the following is a copper-releasing IUD?
explanation: CuT (Copper T), Cu7, and Multiload 375 are copper-releasing IUDs. These IUDs release copper ions which have spermicidal effect (toxic to sperms) and also increase phagocytosis of sperms within the uterus. Lippes loop is a non-medicated IUD that works by physical presence, while Progestasert and LNG-20 are hormone-releasing IUDs.

elimination_hints.hint: Lippes loop is a non-medicated IUD made of plastic; it does not release copper or any chemical.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying Lippes loop as a medicated IUD.

elimination_hints.hint: Progestasert releases progesterone hormone, not copper. It is a hormone-releasing IUD.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing hormone-releasing IUDs with copper-releasing IUDs.

elimination_hints.hint: LNG-20 releases levonorgestrel (a synthetic progesterone), making it a hormone-releasing IUD, not copper-releasing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between different types of medicated IUDs.

question_text: The oral contraceptive pill 'Saheli' is unique because it:
explanation: Saheli is a non-steroidal oral contraceptive pill developed by CDRI (Central Drug Research Institute), Lucknow. Unlike conventional oral pills that contain steroidal hormones (oestrogen and progesterone), Saheli contains centchroman. It is taken once a week (after an initial loading dose), has very few side effects, and has high contraceptive value. Its non-steroidal nature makes it different from all other oral contraceptive pills.

options.text: Contains a high dose of oestrogen for maximum effectiveness
Must be taken daily for it to work
Is a progesterone-only pill with many side effects
Is a non-steroidal preparation with very few side effects, taken once a week

elimination_hints.hint: Saheli is non-steroidal; it does not contain oestrogen or any steroid hormone.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all oral contraceptives are steroidal.

elimination_hints.hint: Saheli needs to be taken only once a week (after an initial daily dosage period), unlike conventional pills taken daily.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the dosage pattern of conventional pills to Saheli.

elimination_hints.hint: Saheli is non-steroidal (contains centchroman, not progesterone) and has very few side effects.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Saheli with progesterone-only pills.

question_text: Which barrier contraceptive is used by females?
explanation: Diaphragms, cervical caps, and female condoms are barrier contraceptives used by females. They cover the cervix during coitus and prevent sperms from entering the uterus. Male condoms are barrier methods used by males. Diaphragms and cervical caps are reusable rubber/silicone devices that are inserted into the vagina to cover the cervix.

elimination_hints.hint: Male condom is a barrier method used by males, not females.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between male and female barrier devices.

elimination_hints.hint: Lippes loop is an IUD (intra-uterine device), not a barrier method. It is placed inside the uterus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying IUDs as barrier methods.

elimination_hints.hint: CuT is a copper-releasing IUD, not a barrier contraceptive. It is placed inside the uterus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing IUDs with barrier contraceptives.

question_text: Hormone-releasing IUDs make the uterus unsuitable for implantation and the cervix hostile to sperms. Which of the following is a hormone-releasing IUD?
explanation: Progestasert and LNG-20 are hormone-releasing IUDs. Progestasert releases progesterone while LNG-20 releases levonorgestrel (a synthetic progestogen). These hormones make the uterine environment unsuitable for implantation and the cervical mucus hostile to sperms. Lippes loop is a non-medicated IUD, while CuT and Multiload 375 are copper-releasing IUDs.

elimination_hints.hint: Lippes loop is a non-medicated IUD; it does not release any hormone or chemical substance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing non-medicated with hormone-releasing IUDs.

elimination_hints.hint: Multiload 375 is a copper-releasing IUD, not a hormone-releasing one.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not differentiating between copper-releasing and hormone-releasing IUDs.

elimination_hints.hint: Cu7 releases copper ions, not hormones. The 'Cu' in its name stands for copper.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing 'Cu' as indicating copper content.

question_text: Coitus interruptus as a contraceptive method involves:
explanation: Coitus interruptus (withdrawal method) is a natural method of contraception in which the male partner withdraws the penis from the vagina just before ejaculation to avoid insemination. This prevents sperms from being deposited in the female reproductive tract. It is one of the oldest known methods of contraception but has a relatively high failure rate due to the possibility of pre-ejaculatory fluid containing sperms.

options.text: Avoiding coitus during the fertile period of the menstrual cycle
Withdrawal of the penis before ejaculation during coitus
Using a chemical spermicide during coitus

elimination_hints.hint: This describes the rhythm method (periodic abstinence), not coitus interruptus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing two different natural methods of contraception.

elimination_hints.hint: Chemical spermicides are chemical methods, not natural methods like coitus interruptus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up natural and chemical methods of contraception.

elimination_hints.hint: Blocking the vas deferens is vasectomy, a surgical/permanent method, not coitus interruptus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing natural methods with surgical sterilization.

question_text: Which of the following statements about IUDs is INCORRECT?
explanation: IUDs are ideal contraceptives for females who want to delay pregnancy or space children. The statement that IUDs provide protection against STDs is incorrect. IUDs do not protect against sexually transmitted diseases. Only barrier methods like condoms provide some protection against STDs. IUDs work by affecting sperm motility, creating a hostile uterine environment, or preventing implantation, but they do not prevent the transmission of pathogens.

options.text: Copper-releasing IUDs increase phagocytosis of sperms within the uterus
Hormone-releasing IUDs make the cervix hostile to sperms
IUDs provide effective protection against sexually transmitted diseases
Non-medicated IUDs like Lippes loop increase phagocytosis of sperms

elimination_hints.hint: This is a correct statement; copper ions released by Cu-IUDs are toxic to sperms and increase phagocytosis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the mechanism of action of copper-releasing IUDs.

elimination_hints.hint: This is a correct statement; hormones from these IUDs alter cervical mucus making it hostile to sperm penetration.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding how hormone-releasing IUDs work.

elimination_hints.hint: This is a correct statement; even non-medicated IUDs increase phagocytosis of sperms in the uterus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking only medicated IUDs affect sperm viability.

question_text: Oral contraceptive pills primarily prevent pregnancy by:
explanation: Oral contraceptive pills contain synthetic progesterone or a combination of progesterone and oestrogen. Their primary mechanism of action is inhibition of ovulation and implantation. The hormones in the pills provide negative feedback on the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, suppressing the release of gonadotropins (FSH and LH), thereby preventing ovulation. They also alter the uterine lining, making it unsuitable for implantation.

options.text: Killing the sperms inside the female reproductive tract
Changing the pH of the uterus to kill the embryo

elimination_hints.hint: Oral pills do not have spermicidal action; they work by hormonal suppression of ovulation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the mechanism of spermicides with that of hormonal pills.

elimination_hints.hint: Physical blocking of fallopian tubes is tubectomy (surgical method), not the mechanism of oral pills.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing hormonal and surgical contraceptive mechanisms.

elimination_hints.hint: Oral pills do not work by altering uterine pH; they prevent ovulation through hormonal feedback mechanisms.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inventing a non-existent mechanism for oral contraceptives.

question_text: Condoms are one of the most popular contraceptives because they:
explanation: Condoms are the most popular barrier contraceptives. A major advantage of condoms over other contraceptive methods is that they not only prevent pregnancy but also protect against sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) including AIDS. They are made of thin rubber/latex and are available for both males and females. They are easily accessible, disposable, and do not require medical supervision.

options.text: Have a 100% success rate in preventing pregnancy
Protect against both unwanted pregnancy and STDs including AIDS

elimination_hints.hint: While condoms are affordable, their popularity is primarily due to dual protection against pregnancy and STDs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing popularity solely to cost rather than dual protection.

elimination_hints.hint: No contraceptive method has a 100% success rate. Condoms can fail due to tearing or improper use.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the effectiveness of condoms.

elimination_hints.hint: Condoms are physical barrier devices made of rubber/latex; they do not contain or release hormones.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing barrier methods with hormonal methods.

question_text: Which of the following contraceptive methods is correctly matched with its type?
explanation: Cervical cap is correctly classified as a barrier method. It is a dome-shaped rubber/silicone device that is placed over the cervix to prevent sperms from entering the uterus. Saheli is a non-steroidal oral contraceptive (not a barrier). CuT is an IUD (not an oral contraceptive). Vasectomy is a surgical method (not a natural method).

elimination_hints.hint: Saheli is a non-steroidal oral contraceptive pill, not a barrier method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly classifying oral pills as barrier methods.

elimination_hints.hint: CuT is an intra-uterine device (IUD), not an oral contraceptive. It is inserted into the uterus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing IUDs with oral contraceptives.

elimination_hints.hint: Vasectomy is a surgical (permanent) method involving cutting of vas deferens, not a natural method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Classifying surgical sterilization as a natural method.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Lactational amenorrhoea is effective as a contraceptive method only up to a maximum of six months after parturition.
Reason (R): After six months, the levels of prolactin decrease and ovulation may resume even if the mother continues breastfeeding.
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Lactational amenorrhoea is effective only up to about six months following delivery because the high prolactin levels during intense lactation suppress GnRH, FSH, and LH secretion, thereby preventing ovulation. After six months, even with continued breastfeeding, the prolactin-mediated suppression of ovulation may weaken, and ovulation can resume, making this method unreliable beyond that period.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains why the method fails after six months — prolactin levels decrease and ovulation resumes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the causal relationship between prolactin decline and loss of contraceptive effect.

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually accurate; prolactin levels do decrease over time, allowing ovulation to resume.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the hormonal basis of lactational amenorrhoea.

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is true as per NCERT; the method is reliable only up to six months.
elimination_hints.misconception: Believing lactational amenorrhoea is effective indefinitely.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Copper-releasing IUDs are effective contraceptives because copper ions suppress sperm motility and the fertilising capacity of sperms.
Reason (R): Copper ions released in the uterus also increase phagocytosis of sperms.
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason provides an additional (correct) explanation supporting the assertion. Copper-releasing IUDs like CuT, Cu7, and Multiload 375 release copper ions into the uterine cavity. These copper ions are toxic to sperms — they suppress sperm motility and reduce their fertilising capacity. Additionally, the copper ions enhance the phagocytic activity of leucocytes in the uterus, which engulf and destroy sperms. Both mechanisms contribute to the contraceptive effect.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Phagocytosis of sperms is part of the mechanism by which copper IUDs work, so R does explain A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising phagocytosis as part of the IUD mechanism.

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct — copper ions do increase phagocytosis of sperms in the uterus.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the phagocytic mechanism of copper IUDs.

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is correct — copper ions are indeed toxic to sperms and suppress their motility and fertilising capacity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the spermicidal effect of copper ions.

question_text:
Match the following contraceptive methods with their categories:

Column I:
(i) Lippes loop
(ii) Cervical cap
(iii) Saheli
(iv) Rhythm method

Column II:
(P) Natural method
(Q) Non-medicated IUD
(R) Barrier method
(S) Oral contraceptive
explanation: Lippes loop is a non-medicated IUD made of plastic (i-Q). Cervical cap is a barrier method used by females to cover the cervix (ii-R). Saheli is a non-steroidal oral contraceptive pill developed by CDRI, Lucknow (iii-S). The rhythm method (periodic abstinence) is a natural method based on avoiding coitus during the fertile period (iv-P).

elimination_hints.hint: Lippes loop is an IUD (Q), not a natural method (P). Cervical cap is a barrier (R), not an IUD (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Misclassifying contraceptive categories.

elimination_hints.hint: Lippes loop is not a barrier method (R); it is placed inside the uterus as an IUD (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing IUDs with barrier methods.

elimination_hints.hint: None of these matches are correct — Lippes loop is not oral (S), and cervical cap is not natural (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Random mismatching of contraceptive methods and categories.

question_text:
Match the following IUDs with their type and active substance:

Column I:
(i) CuT
(ii) Progestasert
(iii) Lippes loop
(iv) LNG-20

Column II:
(P) Non-medicated — no active substance
(Q) Copper-releasing — Cu2+ ions
(R) Hormone-releasing — progesterone
(S) Hormone-releasing — levonorgestrel
explanation: CuT (Copper T) is a copper-releasing IUD that releases Cu2+ ions which are spermicidal (i-Q). Progestasert releases the hormone progesterone (ii-R). Lippes loop is a non-medicated IUD made of polyethylene plastic with no active chemical substance (iii-P). LNG-20 releases levonorgestrel, a synthetic progestogen (iv-S).

elimination_hints.hint: CuT releases copper (Q), not progesterone (R). The 'Cu' in CuT stands for copper.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising the naming convention of copper IUDs.

elimination_hints.hint: Lippes loop is non-medicated (P), not copper-releasing (Q). CuT is copper-releasing, not non-medicated.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the properties of Lippes loop and CuT.

elimination_hints.hint: CuT does not release levonorgestrel (S), and Progestasert is not non-medicated (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Completely mismatching IUDs with their active substances.

question_text:
Statement: Vaults and diaphragms are barrier contraceptive devices used by females that cover the cervix during coitus.

Is this statement True or False?
explanation: This statement is True. Diaphragms, cervical caps, and vaults are barrier contraceptive devices designed for use by females. They are made of rubber and are inserted into the vagina to cover the cervix before coitus, thereby preventing sperms from entering the uterus and reaching the ovum. They are often used with spermicidal creams or jellies to increase their effectiveness.

elimination_hints.hint: Diaphragms and vaults are indeed barrier devices used by females to cover the cervix, as stated in NCERT.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that vaults and diaphragms are female barrier devices.

question_text:
Statement: Injectable contraceptives and implants work by the same hormonal mechanism as oral contraceptive pills — they contain progestogen alone or in combination with oestrogen.

Is this statement True or False?
explanation: This statement is True. Injectable contraceptives (like Depo-Provera) and sub-dermal implants (like Norplant) work on the same principle as oral contraceptive pills. They contain progestogens (synthetic progesterone) alone or in combination with oestrogen. These hormones are released slowly over an extended period, providing long-term contraception by inhibiting ovulation, altering the endometrium, and thickening cervical mucus. Their advantage over pills is that they do not require daily administration.

elimination_hints.hint: Injectables and implants do indeed use the same hormonal principles as oral pills but with different delivery mechanisms.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking that different delivery methods imply different mechanisms of action.

question_text: The non-steroidal oral contraceptive pill __________, developed by __________ (Lucknow), contains centchroman and is taken once a week.
explanation: Saheli is a non-steroidal oral contraceptive pill developed by the Central Drug Research Institute (CDRI), Lucknow. It contains centchroman (ormeloxifene) as its active ingredient. Unlike conventional oral pills that use steroidal hormones, Saheli is non-steroidal, has very few side effects, and has high contraceptive value. It is taken once a week after an initial loading dose, making it more convenient than daily pills.

elimination_hints.hint: Mala-D is a different contraceptive pill (steroidal), and AIIMS is a hospital, not a research institute that developed Saheli.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing different oral contraceptive brands and their origins.

elimination_hints.hint: While the name Saheli is correct, it was developed by CDRI (Central Drug Research Institute), not ICMR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing CDRI with ICMR.

elimination_hints.hint: Centchroman is the active ingredient in Saheli, not the pill's name. Also, Saheli was developed by CDRI, not WHO.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the active ingredient with the product name.

question_text: A 28-year-old married woman with two children wants a reliable, long-term, reversible contraceptive method. She is lactating and does not want to take daily pills. She has no history of copper allergy or hormonal sensitivity. Which of the following would be the MOST appropriate recommendation?
explanation: For a woman seeking long-term, reversible contraception who does not want daily pills and has no contraindications, a copper-releasing IUD like CuT would be the most appropriate choice. CuT provides long-term protection (up to 10 years), is reversible (can be removed when pregnancy is desired), does not require daily compliance, is non-hormonal (suitable during lactation), and has high efficacy. Tubectomy is permanent (not reversible). Daily oral pills do not meet her preference. Lactational amenorrhoea is only effective for 6 months.

options.text: Copper-releasing IUD (CuT) for long-term reversible contraception

elimination_hints.hint: Tubectomy is a permanent (irreversible) surgical method; the patient wants a reversible method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between permanent and reversible contraceptive methods.

elimination_hints.hint: The patient specifically does not want to take daily pills, ruling out oral contraceptives.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring patient preferences in contraceptive counselling.

elimination_hints.hint: Lactational amenorrhoea is effective only up to 6 months and is not a reliable long-term method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the duration and reliability of lactational amenorrhoea.
